speaker: interviewer
text: --talk to your children about your experiences in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. When we came here, we had them. And we saw that other kids-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] want them, they should be like the other kids, happy, go to school. And we knew that, somehow, they were going to read. They were going to get told, and they'll know. They'll know what sort of-- but-- because when they had children, even named them after my-- mostly after my husband's brother, sister, aunts, cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew that they were killed in the war. I mean, they were in school. But special, no. It-- I don't think it's only us. I think most of them wanted to leave-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] leave it out. Do you think losing your parents, so young, and going through what you had to go through, by yourself, affected the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was more, maybe, was afraid for them, it should happen to them. Whenever they used to go out, I had to know where they are or they had to call me. In school, if there's-- it ends around 10 o'clock. I was there with my husband to pick them up. And a few times, they didn't come in time. I was very, very emotional, [INAUDIBLE] they put on me border. My older one didn't show up. She went with her friends. She was older already.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my husband went and looked for her until he found. And even when I was in the country, with them, in the summer, I was very much afraid when it was dark, when they weren't at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we watched our kids. We weren't thinking to much of holidays to leave our kids. I used to say, when I close my doors, and I see them in the house, that's my holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have bad dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. Sometimes. Well, it's 50 years. But maybe, now it comes more than in the younger years, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things do you dream?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my home. I remember my father especially when I do things I always do, like little things. We were like-- my-- our house was always open. And the-- and the-- my friends, I had a lot of friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were sitting and talking. My father stood beside the table. And he doesn't do-- Then they left, he said, you know, it's not nice. What's not nice? You we're talking about somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I wasn't talking, because I would be afraid to talk. Somebody else did this. Yet to listen is just as bad as the talk. So I always remember. I remember then, by that time, girls start to smoke, in-- even at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being popular, I can say, and so, why not? Try a cigarette. When he saw it, his expression on the face was, why, what? He couldn't understand. I said-- I think I gave him that, it's my tooth that is hurting. So that'll stop the--

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, put away, right away, the cigarette. I put away. And you know, when I came here in Canada, a lot of people were smoking. Whenever I wanted to reach for a cigarette, I remembered he-- his expression on his face. And I never did that. I never smoked.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's probably the good things what you remember. Never, never say that you don't have a place for somebody if they want to sleep over. You-- the kids, my kids were used to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody comes. They took the blanket and went downstairs. Because you have to give the place for somebody. So those good things, I think it's rewarding to not be selfish. But you learn at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And plain, we're plain, because nothing-- nothing in this world is to be-- even somebody who has money, what's money? To think that I'm better than somebody else, other things. And that-- Are you religious, Mrs. Snow? Yeah. I'm not a-- ortho-- my house is kosher. You know, certain things I don't do at Shabbos, but I do drive. So I'm not going to put myself in the-- when the kids grow up, they used to want to make class on Saturday. Used to see always kids. Today, Shabbos, by God, just like that. And they knew what this meant.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they probably was writing. They probably, you know, like other-- And they-- they are religious in a way. They all belong to a synagogue. The youngest one, the husband is more religious. My son, too. I used to take him. My husband used to go to store, but I used to take him every Saturday to the synagogue. Because he was a boy, to build in him something in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it mean to you to be Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I-- I look at Salk and [? Starkman, ?] who-- who discovered all those things to help people. I think [? Starkman's ?] streptomycin to-- to-- to heal the lungs. I'm proud. I look at those people, who helped so many people. I was in the hospital, once, when I had a kid. And a nurse told me, you know-- I said, I'm Jewish. She probably knew before I told her. She said, you know what, a Jewish doctor saved me. I don't know who the nurse is. I don't who the doctor is. But the way she said it, so I'm-- I'm proud. Even maybe people look down at us. But we had some good people, who do good work.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had a message to leave your grandchildren, what would you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to be good, to be kind. No matter how much education they have, they shouldn't think that they are better from the ordinary people. You know, like I said, when they made a party for me, I was 65. And they said, mom, make a wish. I made a wish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they said, can we we know what the wish is? I said, sure. So I said to them, I hope, when you reach my age, you should be surrounded with children like you are. And other things, you have no control of your children. You can teach them. You can be good to them. But most of them, if you give them love, they have love in them. And they give to their children. We never had secrets from our kids. Always, if you can, the best for them, if you can help them. They-- they all work. They make a nice living. They never came to us. If we want to give them, we give them. Mom, why? Don't say, why. Why did you want to tell us your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kids wanted us to tell the story. And my husband, as he's not so healthy, he was afraid. And I was thinking, why should I say if he won't tell? But when he said, he'll tell, then I did agree. But I don't know if it will help anybody. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mrs. Snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. This is a picture from my father, myself, my sister, and my brother. That was after my mother passed away. So my cousin, from Pinsk, took the picture. Somehow, it was in Los Angeles. This-- that-- that's the only one she had. The only one I-- we have from our family. On the left is myself and my father and my brother, Milton, and my sister, Itke.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was your father's name? My father's name was Markel Turkienicz. And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Behind our house. It's the back of our house. This window is the kitchen window. I am between those two teachers in the middle. On my right is my cousin Chava Basewicz. On the top row, the third one, on the right side is my cousin, Mottle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basewicz. And on the left, the third one is my cousin, Itke Turkienicz.

speaker: interviewee
text: When was this picture taken? That was taken probably 1935 or '36. And there is, on the left, the-- a Jewish teacher, who taught religion in class. We used to have, once a week, religion. What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Zalman. ?] I don't remember his second name. And the rest were-- And this is a school picture. My younger-- my sister is in there. She's the fifth from that right. And also, we have here the rabbi's son. He's the-- the second to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [? Label ?] Millner. And he was a rabbi already in our town. And he taught religion in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's name is Itke. And some teachers and all the kids in the class. And this is a close-up from the same picture before. The fourth is my sister, Itke. The fourth from the right is my sister, Itke. And the second is my cousin, Itke.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her last name was Basewicz. This is my cousin. She's my cousin from my mother's side. Her name is Yentel. And the-- and the man is Mottel Izelman. They married. They were a couple. They had a little baby. But they were killed. I don't have the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you think this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in '38, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pinsk. This-- this picture is four of my-- four of my cousins from Pinsk. On my right is-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I went blank. One second. Close it. If-- my right, is my cousin, Jaccov, and then Miriam. Jaccov was killed by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Izelman. And Miriam was in Israel. She passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her last name, I don't know, just Izelman. And next is Fraydle Izelman. She died in 1936. And by the end is Mottle Izelman. He was killed by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in probably '34. And this is my cousin, Aidel Izelman, with her husband. They were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken probably in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pinsk. And this picture was taken in 1938. On the bottom, on the right is [? Simka ?] Rosensweig. Then [NON-ENGLISH] I think. I don't know the second name. And next is [NON-ENGLISH]. On the top, on the right, is Purim, [? Schmuel ?] Purim, and [NON-ENGLISH]. Next is me. And then is available [NON-ENGLISH] Turkienicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are all friends of mine. Again, on the top, to my right, is [? Lazer ?] [? Erdmann, ?] [NON-ENGLISH], [? Bel ?] [NON-ENGLISH], and [? Asa-- ?] I don't remember his second name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of them survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Purim, [? Schmuel ?] Purim I mentioned a few. [NON-ENGLISH] died in Israel. And [? Leon ?] [? Erdmann, ?] the first one, to my right, in the top, is still alive in Florida. And [NON-ENGLISH] passed away two years ago, on the bottom, in the middle. And this picture was taken in Bindermichl in 1946. It's me and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Majer Snow. This picture was taken 1995. On the left is my grandson, Lawrence then my son-in-law, Eddie, my granddaughter, Andrea, my son-in-law, Michael, my grandson, Jonathan, my granddaughter, Bonnie, my son-in-law, Alvin, and my son, Allan Mark. And then to my left is my granddaughter, Becky, my daughter, Toby, my daughter, Betty, me, my husband, my daughter, Ann, my daughter-in-law, Susan, and my granddaughter, Candice. On the bottom is my grandson, Elliot, my grandson, Aaron, my grandson, Zackary, my granddaughter, Amanda, my grandson, Jason, and my grandson, Kevin. This is my family. This is my grandson, Darrell Isenberg. And we lost him in a car accident. I can't describe the pain that stays with us and will stay till the rest of our life. He'll be always a part of our family. On my right, is my husband, Majer. Then is my daughter, Betty. In the back is my son, Allan Mark. And there's my daughter, Toby Milian, and my daughter, Ann Isenberg. I should say, Betty Clarfield, but I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. Did someone want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'd-- I'd just like to say that I'm very proud of my parents for being able to have the strength to-- to do this taping today. They've They've always been a source of inspiration to us. And we-- we are very proud that they were able to survive these circumstances. And-- and we're just a very beautiful family and very proud of what they've done.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'd like to thank the Shoah Foundation for giving us this opportunity to-- to tell our story, to tell the story of our family, so that no one will ever forget it. And it will be remembered. Allan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I-- I'd just like to reiterate what my sister said, and that is thanking the Shoah Foundation for allowing my parents to tell their story and making it available for not only their children but for their grandchildren and their descendants. I want to thank you, once again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
